title: Pythagoras's Theorem
author: Pythagoras
hook: You need to build a ramp. The step is 3 feet high, the base starts 4 feet away. How long is the ramp?
hookAction: Drag the sides of the triangle to see a² + b² always equals c².

lessons.instruction: Grab the blue dot on side a and drag to make it longer.
lessons.hint: Find the blue handle at the top of side a.
lessons.insight: The hypotenuse c grew too. The squares always balance: a² + b² = c².

lessons.instruction: Watch the squares. Blue + amber always equals red. Drag both a and b.
lessons.insight: a² + b² = c². Always. No matter what size.

lessons.instruction: Set a = 3 and b = 4. What is c?
lessons.insight: c = 5. The famous 3-4-5 triangle. Builders have used this for 4,000 years.

glossary.tooltip: The longest side, opposite the right angle
Area squares attached to each side
The ramp length equals the hypotenuse

title: Logarithms
author: John Napier
hook: An earthquake at 7.0 vs 8.0 on the Richter scale — it's not 'a little bigger'. It's 10× more powerful.
hookAction: Drag x and y to see how log turns multiplication into addition.

lessons.instruction: Drag x to change the first number. Watch the bars.
lessons.insight: log(100) is only 2. Logarithms compress huge ranges into small numbers.

lessons.instruction: Change both. Verify log(x) + log(y) = log(x×y).
lessons.insight: The product rule: log turns multiplication into addition. Before calculators, this is how people did arithmetic.

lessons.instruction: Set x=10, y=10. Then try x=100, y=100.
lessons.insight: Each +1 on Richter means 10× more powerful. Magnitude 8 isn't 'slightly bigger' than 7.

title: Calculus
author: Newton
hook: Your speedometer is broken. You only have the odometer. How do you figure out your speed?
hookAction: Grab the blue dot and drag it along the curve to see speed as steepness.

lessons.instruction: Grab the blue dot and drag it along the curve.
lessons.insight: The orange line tilts as you move. Its steepness IS the speed — that's a derivative.

lessons.instruction: Drag h smaller. Watch the orange line approach the gray tangent.
lessons.insight: As h → 0, the secant becomes the tangent. That's what 'limit' means — just zooming in.

lessons.instruction: Find where the slope is zero — the flat spots.
lessons.insight: Slope = 0 at peaks and valleys. Setting derivative to zero finds maximums and minimums.

glossary.tooltip: Line through two points on the curve
Line touching the curve at exactly one point
How steep the curve is at this point
The rate of change — slope at a point

title: Law of Gravity
author: Newton
hook: Astronauts don't escape gravity — they fall sideways. The force barely changes at space-station altitude.
hookAction: Drag the masses to see how gravity changes.

lessons.instruction: Drag m₁ in the formula to make the first mass heavier.
lessons.insight: More mass = more gravitational pull. That's why Jupiter has such strong gravity.

lessons.instruction: Drag r downward. Make the objects closer.
lessons.insight: Halve the distance, quadruple the force. The r² makes gravity extremely strong up close.

lessons.instruction: Maximize the force. Max masses, min distance.
lessons.insight: Huge masses + tiny distance = enormous force. This is black-hole territory.

glossary.tooltip: Shows direction and strength of gravity
Gap between the centers of the two masses

title: Wave Equation
author: J. d'Alembert
hook: Pluck a guitar string. Why a musical note instead of noise? Only certain wave shapes fit.
hookAction: Adjust frequency and amplitude to see how waves behave.

variables.name: freq
variables.description: Frequency — how fast the wave oscillates

variables.name: amp
variables.description: Amplitude — how tall the wave is

lessons.instruction: Drag frequency higher. Watch the waves compress.
lessons.insight: Higher frequency = more waves per second = higher pitch in sound.

lessons.instruction: Now change amplitude. The wave gets taller or shorter.
lessons.insight: Amplitude is loudness in sound, brightness in light. The wave shape stays the same.

lessons.instruction: Watch the red superposition — two waves combining.
lessons.insight: When waves combine, they add up. This is how noise-canceling headphones work — by adding an opposite wave.

title: The Square Root of Minus One
author: Euler
hook: Your phone screen can rotate 90°. The software multiplies every coordinate by i.
hookAction: Tap 'Multiply by i' and watch every point rotate exactly 90°.

lessons.instruction: Drag a to move the point left and right on the real axis.
lessons.insight: The real part moves horizontally, like a normal number line. Complex numbers add a second dimension.

lessons.instruction: Now drag b to move the point up and down.
lessons.insight: The imaginary axis is perpendicular to the real axis. Every point is a complex number a + bi.

lessons.instruction: Set a=1, b=0, then use 'Multiply by i' button.
lessons.insight: Multiplying by i rotates 90°. Do it twice (i×i) = 180° = multiplying by -1. That's why i² = -1!

title: Euler's Formula for Polyhedra
author: Euler
hook: Count corners, edges, and faces of any 3D shape. Corners − edges + faces always equals 2.
hookAction: Tap different shapes and try to break the rule.

lessons.instruction: Look at the cube: 8 corners, 12 edges, 6 faces. Does 8-12+6 = 2?
lessons.insight: 8 - 12 + 6 = 2. It works!

lessons.instruction: Click other shapes. Count V, E, F for each.
lessons.insight: V - E + F = 2 for every convex polyhedron. Euler proved this in 1751.

lessons.instruction: Try the icosahedron: 12 vertices, 30 edges, 20 faces.
lessons.insight: 12 - 30 + 20 = 2. Still works! This is a topological invariant — it doesn't change no matter how you stretch the shape.

title: Normal Distribution
author: C. F. Gauss
hook: In a class of 1000 students, how many score above 90%? This curve answers that.
hookAction: Drag the center and width of the curve to see how scores spread.

variables.name: mu
variables.description: Mean — center of the bell curve

variables.name: sigma
variables.description: Standard deviation — width of the spread

lessons.instruction: Drag μ to shift the curve left and right.
lessons.insight: μ is the average. Shifting it moves the entire bell curve.

lessons.instruction: Drag σ to make the curve wider or narrower.
lessons.insight: Bigger σ = more spread. Smaller σ = everyone clustered around the mean.

lessons.instruction: Set σ=1. The shaded region is 68% of all data.
lessons.insight: 68% within 1σ, 95% within 2σ, 99.7% within 3σ. Works for heights, test scores, manufacturing — everything.

title: Fourier Transform
author: J. Fourier
hook: Every sound your phone plays is just a recipe of pure tones mixed together.
hookAction: Toggle harmonics on/off to build complex sounds from simple waves.

lessons.instruction: Set all harmonics to 0 except A₁. That's a pure sine wave.
lessons.insight: A pure sine wave is the simplest possible sound. Every complex sound is built from these.

lessons.instruction: Now add A₂ and A₃. Watch the shape change.
lessons.insight: Adding harmonics creates timbre — why a piano and violin sound different playing the same note.

lessons.instruction: Try the 'Square-ish' preset. Odd harmonics make square waves.
lessons.insight: A perfect square wave needs infinite harmonics. This is how synthesizers and MP3 compression work.

title: Navier-Stokes Equation
author: Navier, Stokes
hook: Why does smoke curl? Why do planes fly? This equation governs every fluid — and we still can't fully solve it.
hookAction: Adjust viscosity and flow speed to watch fluid behavior change.

variables.name: viscosity
variables.description: Viscosity — how thick/sticky the fluid is

lessons.instruction: Watch the particles flow around the obstacle.
lessons.insight: The particles follow streamlines — paths that the fluid naturally takes.

lessons.instruction: Increase viscosity. The flow becomes smoother.
lessons.insight: High viscosity = honey. Low viscosity = water. The fluid resists deformation more.

lessons.instruction: Try 'Turbulent' preset. Low viscosity + high speed = chaos.
lessons.insight: This transition from smooth to chaotic flow is why Navier-Stokes is a million-dollar unsolved problem.

title: Maxwell's Equations
author: J. C. Maxwell
hook: How does WiFi get through walls? Electric fields shaking back and forth launch waves at the speed of light.
hookAction: Place charges and watch field lines appear. Switch to wave mode.

lessons.instruction: Click to place charges. Watch field lines appear.
lessons.insight: Electric field lines show the force direction. They flow from positive to negative charges.

lessons.instruction: Switch to Wave mode. See E and B fields oscillating.
lessons.insight: E and B fields create each other — a changing E makes B, a changing B makes E. This self-sustaining loop IS light.

lessons.instruction: Change wavelength. Shorter = higher energy (X-rays). Longer = radio.
lessons.insight: All electromagnetic radiation — radio, microwave, light, X-rays, gamma — is the same thing, just different wavelengths.

title: Second Law of Thermodynamics
author: L. Boltzmann
hook: Why can't you unscramble an egg? The odds are 1 in a number with more digits than atoms in the universe.
hookAction: Drag temperature and watch order dissolve into chaos.

variables.name: temperature
variables.description: Temperature — how much energy the particles have

lessons.instruction: Set temperature to 0. Look at the ordered particles on the left.
lessons.insight: At low temperature, particles stay organized. Low entropy = high order.

lessons.instruction: Drag temperature up. Watch the right panel get chaotic.
lessons.insight: More energy = particles scatter randomly. Entropy (disorder) increases.

lessons.instruction: Set to maximum. This always happens — never the reverse.
lessons.insight: Entropy always increases. This gives time its direction — the 'arrow of time'. You can't unscramble an egg.

title: Relativity
author: Einstein
hook: GPS satellites need Einstein's corrections. Without them, your location drifts 10 km per day.
hookAction: Drag speed to see time slow down and objects shrink.

variables.name: v
variables.description: Velocity as fraction of light speed

lessons.instruction: Drag v up slowly. Watch the Lorentz factor γ.
lessons.insight: At low speeds, γ ≈ 1 — time and space are normal. Relativity only matters at extreme speeds.

lessons.instruction: Push v to 0.9c. Watch time dilation and length contraction.
lessons.insight: At 90% light speed, time runs 2.3× slower. A 10-year trip feels like 4.4 years. The twin paradox is real.

lessons.instruction: Try to reach v = 1.0. You can't.
lessons.insight: γ goes to infinity as v → c. You'd need infinite energy to reach light speed. Nothing with mass can get there.

title: Schrodinger's Equation
author: E. Schrodinger
hook: An electron isn't a ball — it's a probability cloud. Confine it more, and it gets MORE energetic.
hookAction: Change quantum number n and box width L to see the wave function change.

lessons.instruction: This is n=1, the ground state. The blue wave shows where the particle is likely to be.
lessons.insight: A quantum particle doesn't have a definite position. The wave function gives probabilities.

lessons.instruction: Increase n. Watch the wave function develop more peaks.
lessons.insight: Higher n = more nodes = higher energy. Energy goes as n².

lessons.instruction: Shrink the box (L). Watch energy levels spread apart.
lessons.insight: Heisenberg Uncertainty: confine the particle more → energy increases. This is why atoms don't collapse.

title: Information Theory
author: C. Shannon
hook: Playing 20 Questions? If the answer is almost certainly 'yes', the question was useless.
hookAction: Drag probability to see how uncertainty changes.

lessons.instruction: Set p = 0.5. This is maximum uncertainty.
lessons.insight: H = 1 bit at p = 0.5. A fair coin carries the most information — you genuinely don't know what's coming.

lessons.instruction: Push p toward 0.99. Watch entropy drop to nearly zero.
lessons.insight: Near-certain outcomes carry almost no information. 'Will the sun rise?' tells you nothing — you already knew.

lessons.instruction: Find the symmetric shape of the entropy curve.
lessons.insight: H(p) = H(1-p). Knowing something is 90% likely or 10% likely gives the same amount of information. Entropy measures surprise.

title: Chaos Theory
author: R. May
hook: Weather forecasts fail past 10 days. The tiniest change leads to completely different outcomes.
hookAction: Drag r and watch order dissolve into chaos.

variables.name: r
variables.description: Growth parameter — controls stability vs chaos

lessons.instruction: Set r ≈ 2.8. The time series settles to one value.
lessons.insight: At low r, the system finds equilibrium. Like a stable ecosystem.

lessons.instruction: Set r ≈ 3.2. Now it oscillates between two values.
lessons.insight: Period-2 cycle: the population overshoots, then undershoots. Predictable but not fixed.

lessons.instruction: Push r to 3.8. Welcome to chaos.
lessons.insight: Looks random but it's deterministic. This is chaos: predictable rules, unpredictable behavior.

lessons.instruction: Change x₀ by just 0.01. Watch trajectories diverge.
lessons.insight: The butterfly effect. A butterfly in Brazil can change Texas weather. That's why forecasts fail past 10 days.

title: Black-Scholes Equation
author: Black, Scholes
hook: You can buy the RIGHT to purchase a stock at today's price, but in the future. How much is that right worth?
hookAction: Drag volatility and time to see how option prices change.

variables.name: K
variables.description: Strike price — the agreed buy/sell price

variables.name: sigma
variables.description: Volatility — how much the stock price swings

lessons.instruction: Drag K. Higher strike = cheaper call option (you'd pay more to exercise).
lessons.insight: Call = right to BUY. Higher strike means you pay more to exercise, so the right is worth less.

lessons.instruction: Drag volatility up. Both call and put get more expensive.
lessons.insight: More volatility = more valuable options. If the stock could go way up, you profit. If it goes down, you just don't exercise.

lessons.instruction: Drag T toward zero. Watch the curve sharpen.
lessons.insight: As expiry approaches, time value evaporates. The smooth curve collapses into a sharp corner. This is theta decay.
